county_metadata.record_losses: No known major record losses. Records intact from county formation in 1902. However, parent county Sumter lost pre-1801 records in a fire, so researchers tracing Lee County families back further must account for that gap.
county_metadata.jurisdictional_notes: Formed 1902 from portions of Sumter, Darlington, and Kershaw counties. Named for Robert E. Lee. One of SC's youngest counties. Pre-1902 records are split among three parent counties.
county_metadata.research_tips: Lee is a young county (1902) so pre-formation research requires checking Sumter (burned — 1801 fire), Darlington (burned — 1806 fire), and Kershaw (intact from 1791) records. The online deed portal at leencrod.org provides access to modern records. Lee County Historical Society has some local resources. Bishopville was incorporated in 1910. For the Lynchburg area within Lee County, check Sumter County records. FamilySearch has marriage records and some probate microfilm.
county_metadata.online_land_records_scope: Deeds and property records from county formation (1902) to present. Online portal.

source_profiles.source_name: Lee County Register of Deeds Online Records
source_profiles.coverage_notes: Deed records from county formation. Free online portal.

source_profiles.repository: FamilySearch / SC Department of Archives and History
source_profiles.coverage_notes: Probate records from county formation. Pre-1902 probate split among Sumter, Darlington, and Kershaw.
Lee first appears in federal census in 1910 (formed 1902). Pre-1902 census under Sumter, Darlington, and Kershaw counties.

county_metadata.record_losses: Federal troops burned the courthouse and records in February 1865 during Sherman's March. Pre-1839 deed records COMPLETELY LOST. Deed records 1839-1865 partially reconstructed from surviving fragments. Marriage records lost. Some court records survived because they were housed separately or had been copied to state archives.
county_metadata.jurisdictional_notes: Formed 1785 from Orangeburg District. Named for the Battle of Lexington (1775). Converted from district to county in 1868. Lexington was on Sherman's direct route from Columbia to Winnsboro.
county_metadata.research_tips: This is a BURNED COUNTY with severe pre-1839 losses. Start with the online deed portal at lexington.sc.publicsearch.us for post-1865 records. For pre-1865 research: State land grant plats (1784-1868) at archivesindex.sc.gov are essential substitutes. FamilySearch catalog #1125050 and #1163342 have marriage records that postdate the fire. Check Orangeburg District records for the overlap period (1785-1868). The German-Swiss immigration heritage of Lexington District means church records (particularly Lutheran and German Reformed) are critical alternative sources — many survive independently of county records. The Lexington County Museum (Fox House, 231 Fox Street) has some local genealogical materials. The Old Lexington County Genealogical Society is active.
county_metadata.online_land_records_scope: Deed records from 1839 forward (pre-1839 completely lost in 1865 fire). Records 1839-1865 are partially reconstructed. Full coverage from 1865 forward. Free public search portal.
county_metadata.heritage_book: A History of Lexington County, South Carolina by John Hammond Moore (University of South Carolina Press)

source_profiles.source_name: Lexington County Register of Deeds Online (Public Search)
source_profiles.coverage_notes: Pre-1839 deeds completely lost. Records 1839-1865 partially reconstructed from surviving fragments. Complete coverage 1865-present.
source_profiles.survival_notes: Pre-1839 lost in 1865 fire; 1839-1865 partial; 1865+ complete.

source_profiles.repository: SC Department of Archives and History
source_profiles.coverage_notes: State-level plat records for land grants. CRITICAL substitute for completely destroyed pre-1839 county deed records. Part of the 51,055-image statewide collection.
source_profiles.survival_notes: State-held records — not affected by county courthouse fire.

source_profiles.coverage_notes: FamilySearch catalog #1125050. Marriage licenses from SC statewide licensing (1911). Pre-1865 marriage records destroyed in fire.
source_profiles.survival_notes: Post-1911 records; pre-fire marriage records lost.

source_profiles.source_name: Lexington County Marriage Settlements and Contracts
source_profiles.coverage_notes: FamilySearch catalog #1163342. Post-fire marriage settlements and contracts.

source_profiles.source_name: Lexington District Lutheran and German Reformed Church Records
source_profiles.coverage_notes: German-Swiss settlement created extensive Lutheran and German Reformed church records that survive independently of county records. Critical substitute source for the burned county period. St. Michael's Lutheran, St. John's Lutheran, and other congregations have records predating the county.
source_profiles.survival_notes: Church records survived because they were held by individual congregations, not the courthouse.

county_metadata.record_losses: No known major record losses. Records intact from district formation.
county_metadata.jurisdictional_notes: Original formation is complex: created 1785 as Liberty County from Georgetown District, renamed Marion District in 1798 (effective 1800). Named for Revolutionary War hero Francis Marion ('The Swamp Fox'). Florence County carved from Marion in 1888. Dillon County carved from Marion in 1910. Converted from district to county in 1868.
county_metadata.research_tips: Marion's record chain goes back to 1800 when it became a separate district. For pre-1800 records, check Georgetown District. Florence (1888) and Dillon (1910) were both carved from Marion, so researchers tracking families in those areas should check Marion records first. FamilySearch has probate and court records. The Marion County Museum has local history materials. The Pee Dee region genealogical resources at Florence County Library also cover Marion. SC Department of Archives has microfilmed collections.
county_metadata.online_land_records_scope: No free online deed portal found. In-person access at Register of Deeds office: Marion County Courthouse, 2615 E. Highway 76, Suite 203, Marion, SC 29571.
county_metadata.heritage_book: Marion County Heritage (Marion County Historical Society)

source_profiles.repository: FamilySearch / SC Department of Archives and History
source_profiles.coverage_notes: Probate records, estates, wills from district formation. Pre-1800 probate under Georgetown District.

source_profiles.source_name: South Carolina Will Transcripts — Marion District
source_profiles.repository: SC Department of Archives and History
source_profiles.coverage_notes: Part of statewide will transcription project. Searchable by name with images.
Marion District first appears in census in 1800. Note Florence carved out 1888 and Dillon 1910 — census geography shifts.

county_metadata.record_losses: No known major record losses. Records intact from earliest period.
county_metadata.jurisdictional_notes: Formed 1785 from Cheraws District. Named for John Churchill, 1st Duke of Marlborough. Converted from district to county in 1868. Chesterfield County was carved from the Cheraws District alongside Marlboro.
county_metadata.research_tips: Marlboro has excellent early records. FamilySearch catalog #114596 has conveyance records, #8645 has court journals, and #112858 has will records. The Marlboro County Historical Society is based in Bennettsville. The Marlborough Historical Society Museum has local materials. Pre-1785 records are in Cheraws District files at SCDAH. Bennettsville was a significant Pee Dee region town with early records. The county borders North Carolina, so cross-border migration patterns are important — check Anson and Richmond County NC records for connected families.
county_metadata.online_land_records_scope: No free online deed portal found. In-person access at Register of Deeds: Marlboro County Courthouse, 3rd Floor, 205 E. Market Street, Bennettsville, SC 29512.
county_metadata.heritage_book: A History of Marlboro County by Thomas C. Smith (1999)

source_profiles.coverage_notes: FamilySearch catalog #114596. Conveyance (deed) records from county formation.
FamilySearch catalog #8645. Court journals from county formation.
FamilySearch catalog #112858. Will records from county formation.

county_metadata.record_losses: No known major record losses. Records intact from county formation in 1916. However, parent county Abbeville suffered triple courthouse fires (1872-1873), so pre-formation records from the Abbeville portion are affected.
county_metadata.jurisdictional_notes: Formed 1916 from portions of Abbeville, Edgefield, and Greenwood counties. SC's youngest county. Named for Cyrus Hall McCormick, inventor of the mechanical reaper, who had mining interests in the area. Pre-1916 records split among three parent counties.
county_metadata.research_tips: McCormick is SC's youngest and smallest county (1916). Pre-formation research requires checking Abbeville (burned — triple fires), Edgefield (never burned — excellent records), and Greenwood (intact from 1897) records. FamilySearch catalog #1261073 has marriage records. The Abbeville District Historical Association (PO Box 578, McCormick, SC 29835) covers the area. Dorn Gold Mine historical site connects to the McCormick mining history. The town of McCormick was a gold mining center. Check Edgefield County records first for the best pre-1916 coverage since Edgefield never burned.
county_metadata.online_land_records_scope: No free online deed portal found. In-person access at Register of Deeds: McCormick County Courthouse, 133 S. Mine Street, McCormick, SC 29835.

source_profiles.coverage_notes: FamilySearch catalog #1261073. Marriage records from county formation.

source_profiles.repository: FamilySearch / SC Department of Archives and History
source_profiles.coverage_notes: Probate records from county formation. Pre-1916 probate split among Abbeville, Edgefield, and Greenwood.
McCormick first appears in federal census in 1920 (formed 1916). Pre-1916 census under Abbeville, Edgefield, and Greenwood counties.

county_metadata.record_losses: No known major record losses. Records intact from county formation in 1785.
county_metadata.jurisdictional_notes: Formed 1785 from Ninety-Six District along with Laurens, Spartanburg, Union, Abbeville, and Edgefield. Converted from district to county in 1868. Saluda County carved from Edgefield (not Newberry) in 1895, but some boundary adjustments affected the area.
county_metadata.research_tips: Newberry has good record survival. FamilySearch catalog #448459 has will records. Newberry County Historical Society and Museum (1300 Friend Street, Newberry) has local materials. Newberry College Library has some genealogical resources. The Newberry Observer newspaper (1880s-present) is a key local source. German-Swiss settlement in the area means Lutheran church records are important supplements. Check Ninety-Six District records for the overlap period. The O'Neall Law Office Museum has historical legal documents relevant to genealogy.
county_metadata.online_land_records_scope: No free online deed portal found. In-person access at Register of Deeds: Newberry County Courthouse, 1226 College Street, Newberry, SC 29108.
county_metadata.heritage_book: Annals of Newberry by John Belton O'Neall and John A. Chapman (1892, reprinted)

source_profiles.coverage_notes: FamilySearch catalog #448459. Will records from county formation.

source_profiles.source_name: South Carolina Will Transcripts — Newberry District
source_profiles.repository: SC Department of Archives and History
source_profiles.coverage_notes: Part of statewide will transcription project. Searchable by name with images.

source_profiles.repository: FamilySearch / SC Department of Archives and History
source_profiles.coverage_notes: Probate records, estates from county formation through 1977.

county_metadata.record_losses: No known major record losses. Records intact from county formation in 1868.
county_metadata.jurisdictional_notes: Formed 1868 when Pickens District was divided into Oconee (western portion) and Pickens (eastern portion) counties during the 1868 SC Constitution conversion from districts to counties. Before 1826, the area was part of Pendleton District. Named from Cherokee 'Oconee' meaning 'land beside the water.' Walhalla was founded by a German colonization society in 1850.
county_metadata.research_tips: For pre-1868 records, search Pickens District. For pre-1826, search Pendleton District. FamilySearch catalog #1284234 has estate records. The Oconee County ROD website (oconeesc.com) provides online access. The Oconee County Historical Society is active. The German heritage of Walhalla means church records (Lutheran, particularly St. John's Lutheran) extend back to the 1850s. The Mountain Rest area and Stumphouse Tunnel historical site connect to railroad-era records. Keowee-Toxaway State Park interprets Cherokee heritage in the area.
county_metadata.online_land_records_scope: Deeds and property records from county formation (1868) to present. Online portal at county website.
county_metadata.heritage_book: History of Oconee County, South Carolina by Minnie C. Earle (1947)

source_profiles.source_name: Oconee County Register of Deeds Online Records
source_profiles.coverage_notes: Deed records from county formation. Free online portal. Pre-1868 records under Pickens District; pre-1826 under Pendleton District.
FamilySearch catalog #1284234. Estate records from county formation.

source_profiles.repository: FamilySearch / SC Department of Archives and History
source_profiles.coverage_notes: Probate records from county formation through 1977.

county_metadata.record_losses: Federal troops burned the courthouse in February 1865 during Sherman's March. Pre-1865 records largely destroyed, creating a 96-year gap (1769-1865) in county-level documentation. Some scattered records survived. Post-1865 records are substantially complete.
county_metadata.jurisdictional_notes: Formed 1785 from original colonial Orangeburg District (established 1769). One of SC's original colonial districts. Named for William, Prince of Orange. Converted from district to county in 1868. Calhoun County carved from Orangeburg in 1908.
county_metadata.research_tips: This is a BURNED COUNTY with a 96-year gap. Start with post-1865 records at orangeburgscrod.org. For pre-1865 research: State land grant plats (1784-1868) at archivesindex.sc.gov are essential. Will Transcripts (1782-1855) at SCDAH may have some Orangeburg entries. FamilySearch has microfilmed collections. The German-Swiss heritage of early Orangeburg settlers means church records (particularly St. Andrew's Lutheran, Orangeburg) are critical — some date to the 1730s and survived independently of county records. The Orangeburg County Historical Society is active. Claflin University (HBCU, est. 1869) and South Carolina State University (est. 1896) archives have African American community records. The Orangeburg Massacre (1968) records are at SCDAH.
county_metadata.online_land_records_scope: Deed records from 1865 forward (pre-1865 records destroyed in fire). Free online search portal. Post-1865 records are substantially complete.
county_metadata.heritage_book: The History of Orangeburg County, South Carolina by Alexander S. Salley (1898, reprinted)

source_profiles.source_name: Orangeburg County Register of Deeds Online Records
source_profiles.coverage_notes: Post-fire deed records only. Pre-1865 deeds destroyed in 1865 courthouse fire. Free online search portal.

source_profiles.repository: SC Department of Archives and History
source_profiles.coverage_notes: State-level plat records for Orangeburg District land grants. CRITICAL substitute for destroyed county deed records spanning the 96-year gap. Part of the 51,055-image statewide collection.
source_profiles.survival_notes: State-held records — not affected by county courthouse fire.

source_profiles.source_name: St. Andrew's Lutheran Church Records, Orangeburg
source_profiles.coverage_notes: German-Swiss colonial-era church records dating to the 1730s. Survived independently of county records because they were held by the congregation. Critical substitute source for the 96-year burned county gap.
source_profiles.survival_notes: Church-held records — not affected by courthouse fire.

source_profiles.source_name: South Carolina Will Transcripts — Orangeburg District

source_profiles.coverage_notes: Part of statewide will transcription project. Critical for pre-fire Orangeburg research.

county_metadata.record_losses: No known major record losses. Records intact from county formation in 1868. Pre-1868 Pickens District records shared with Oconee County.
county_metadata.jurisdictional_notes: Formed 1868 when Pickens District was divided into Pickens (eastern portion) and Oconee (western portion) counties during the SC Constitution conversion from districts to counties. Before 1826, the area was part of Pendleton District. Named for Revolutionary War hero General Andrew Pickens.
county_metadata.research_tips: For pre-1868 records, search Pickens District (records shared between Pickens and Oconee counties). For pre-1826, search Pendleton District. FamilySearch catalog #2471929 has 1850 census records. The Pickens County ROD at pickensscrod.us provides online access. The Pickens County Historical Society is active. Hagood Mill Historic Site & Folklife Center has local cultural materials. Clemson University (founded 1889 on John C. Calhoun's Fort Hill plantation) Special Collections have regional genealogical materials. The county's Cherokee heritage is significant — Old Pickens (Keowee) was site of Cherokee Fort Prince George.
county_metadata.online_land_records_scope: Deeds and property records from county formation (1868) to present. Free online search portal. Pre-1868 records under Pickens District; pre-1826 under Pendleton District.
county_metadata.heritage_book: History of Pickens County by Mrs. Pearl S. McFall (1963)

source_profiles.source_name: Pickens County Register of Deeds Online Records
source_profiles.coverage_notes: Deed records from county formation. Free online search portal.
FamilySearch catalog #2471929. Census records from 1850 forward. Pre-1868 listed under Pickens District; pre-1826 under Pendleton District.

source_profiles.repository: FamilySearch / SC Department of Archives and History
source_profiles.coverage_notes: Probate records from county formation through 1977. Pre-1868 probate under Pickens District.
